title: Multiplying 10s
description: Practice multiplying 2-digit multiples of 10, such as 50x70=3500.

hints:
$70$ and $30$ are both multiples of $10$.
Let's break them apart:

$\greenD{70} \times \blueD{30} = (\greenD7 \times \greenD{10}) \times (\blueD3 \times \blueD{10})$

We can change the order of the factors to make it easier to multiply:

$\greenD{70} \times \blueD{30} = \greenD7 \times \blueD{3} \times \greenD{10} \times \blueD{10}$

Multiply $7$ and $3$. 
Then multiply $10$ and $10$.

$\begin{align} (\greenD7\times \blueD3) &\times (\greenD{10}  \times \blueD{10})\\\\
21&\times100 \end{align}$

Multiplying by $100$ moves every digit two place values to the left. 

$21 \times 100 = 2{,}100$

$20$ and $30$ are both multiples of $10$.
Let's break them apart:

$\greenD{20} \times \blueD{30} = (\greenD2 \times \greenD{10}) \times (\blueD3 \times \blueD{10})$

We can change the order of the factors to make it easier to multiply:

$\greenD{20} \times \blueD{30} = \greenD2 \times \blueD{3} \times \greenD{10} \times \blueD{10}$

Multiply $2$ and $3$. 
Then multiply $10$ and $10$.

$\begin{align} (\greenD2\times \blueD3) &\times (\greenD{10}  \times \blueD{10})\\\\
6&\times100 \end{align}$

Multiplying by $100$ moves every digit two place values to the left. 

$6 \times 100 = 600$
